title: Davis Square Community Block Party
subtitle: Infrastructure Intelligence for a Fully Accessible, City-Sponsored Community Event
demo_context: Cyvl demo for Boston CIO — showing how infrastructure data enables safe, accessible, community-centered street programming in Somerville, MA

center.display_name: Davis Square, Ball Square, Somerville, Middlesex County, Massachusetts

streets.name: Elm Street
streets.character: Primary commercial corridor through Davis Square; lined with restaurants, bars, and shops
streets.closure_priority: Primary closure zone — heart of the event

streets.name: Highland Avenue
streets.character: Major arterial connecting Davis to Ball Square and beyond
streets.closure_priority: Partial closure / event boundary; requires traffic rerouting

streets.name: College Avenue
streets.character: Secondary commercial street, MBTA Red Line Davis Station access road
streets.closure_priority: Coordinate with MBTA; pedestrian flow route from T station

streets.name: Holland Street
streets.character: Residential / light commercial, leads to Tufts University area
streets.closure_priority: Secondary closure; vendor/parking staging area candidate

pavement_conditions.summary: 200 pavement segments assessed within 500m of Davis Square center
pavement_conditions.overall_assessment: Mixed — majority of segments in Good/Satisfactory range, but significant pockets of Poor/Serious/Failed pavement requiring attention before event

critical_segments.label: Failed
critical_segments.action: Immediate repair required before event — safety risk for wheelchair/mobility users

critical_segments.label: Serious
critical_segments.action: Flag for temporary matting if in event zone

critical_segments.label: Serious
critical_segments.action: Flag for temporary matting if in event zone

event_relevance.parking_signs: Regulatory signs govern all on-street parking — temporary 'No Parking / Event Zone' overlays needed on all streets in closure area
event_relevance.speed_limit_signs: Standard neighborhood speed limits in effect — temporary event signage will supersede
event_relevance.pedestrian_crossing: Warning signs at crosswalks confirm pedestrian infrastructure presence throughout square

Power source access for vendor booths and stage; coordinate with Eversource
Curb inventory confirms edge definitions for barricade placement
Must be marked / protected from heavy equipment and temporary structures
ADA curb ramps confirmed — 21 within 500m radius; critical for accessibility routing
Street lighting confirmed for early evening event activities
Sidewalk segments inventoried — pedestrian flow planning baseline
Must remain unobstructed per DPW requirements
ADA-compliant accessible pedestrian signals at 6 intersections — confirms strong ADA infrastructure
Signal poles available for temporary event signage attachment
Fire hydrant locations — 12-ft clearance mandatory per Somerville event code
Signals can be switched to flashing/pedestrian mode during event
Only 1 documented — supplement with temporary bike corrals for event
Existing camera for event safety monitoring

accessibility_features.demo_talking_point: Cyvl identified 21 ADA ramps in the event zone — city can verify continuity of accessible routes before event day
6 audible pedestrian signal locations confirmed — event layout can route attendees through these intersections
Lighting confirmed for early evening programming

accessibility_features.feature: Davis Square MBTA Red Line Station
accessibility_features.demo_talking_point: Event is transit-accessible; reduces parking demand and supports car-free attendance

hazards_to_address.ada_impact: High — rough surfaces impede wheelchair travel

hazards_to_address.action: Deploy temporary bike corrals — expect high bike attendance at Davis Square events

event_economics.basis: National Endowment for the Arts studies show community events increase local business revenue 25-30%; Davis Square's 200+ businesses and high foot-traffic baseline amplify this effect
event_economics.comparison: ArtBeat 2025 (same location) attracted ~100 vendors and thousands of attendees annually — city-sponsored block party with infrastructure-backed safety planning projects similar attendance
event_economics.job_creation: 30-50 temporary event jobs (staff, security, vendor assistants)
event_economics.tax_revenue: Incremental sales tax on estimated $50,000+ in gross sales

streets: Elm Street (between Holland St and Highland Ave intersection)
primary_closure_zone.rationale: Core commercial corridor with highest density of restaurants, cafes, and shops; Cyvl data shows multiple Good/Satisfactory pavement segments (PCI 80-98) in this zone — ideal for accessible event surface

secondary_closures.street: College Avenue (partial — from Elm St intersection to MBTA entrance)
secondary_closures.rationale: Maintains pedestrian flow from Red Line T station into event; MBTA coordination required

secondary_closures.street: Holland Street (partial — first block from Davis Square)
secondary_closures.rationale: Staging / loading area for vendor setup; separates event logistics from public pedestrian zones

street_closure_plan.traffic_rerouting: Highland Avenue remains open as primary vehicle bypass; Porter Square via Beacon Street and Ball Square via Broadway serve as alternate routes. MBTA Bus Routes 87 and 89 deviate via Highland Avenue during closure period.
street_closure_plan.emergency_access: 12-foot minimum clearance lane maintained on all closed streets per Somerville event code; fire hydrants at 3 documented locations within radius remain fully accessible with 15-foot buffer; EMS staging area designated at Holland Street staging zone
dpw_requirements: All street closure devices supplied by Somerville DPW or specified by Parking Department
No privately owned vehicles used as barricades
Minimum 12-foot emergency vehicle clearance on all closed streets
Catch basins (6 in radius) must remain unobstructed
Manhole covers (22 in radius) protected from heavy equipment

accessibility_plan.philosophy: Cyvl infrastructure data drives every accessibility decision — no guessing, full documentation for ADA compliance

ada_compliant_routes.route: MBTA Red Line Davis Station → Elm Street Event Zone
ada_compliant_routes.description: College Avenue pedestrian corridor from accessible T station (elevator-equipped) to event entrance; ADA ramps confirmed at College Ave / Elm St intersection
ada_compliant_routes.surface_quality: Good pavement on College Ave approach (PCI 85-90 range)
ada_compliant_routes.push_button_signals: Confirmed present at College Ave / Elm St intersection

ada_compliant_routes.route: Highland Avenue parking approach → Event Zone (north entry)
ada_compliant_routes.description: Accessible route from Highland Ave sidewalk to event; ADA ramps inventoried
ada_compliant_routes.surface_quality: Mixed — Fair/Good segments; route planning should prioritize PCI > 70 segments
ada_compliant_routes.push_button_signals: Confirmed present at Highland Ave intersections

ada_compliant_routes.route: Holland Street residential approach → Event Zone (west entry)
ada_compliant_routes.description: Residential neighborhood approach; accessible for attendees with mobility devices
ada_compliant_routes.surface_quality: Good in staging zone area (PCI 87-89 on nearby segments)

accessibility_plan.accessible_restrooms: Portable accessible restrooms required — minimum 2 ADA-compliant units (60" turning radius, grab bars, firm level surface approach). Placement: one near MBTA entrance on College Ave, one mid-Elm Street zone. Existing venue restrooms (Diesel Cafe, Foundry on Elm) serve as supplemental options with staff monitoring for accessibility.
accessibility_plan.surface_remediation: 2 Failed and 12 Serious PCI segments within radius must be repaired, covered with protective matting, or routed around to ensure wheelchair and mobility device access across all event zones. Cyvl segment data provides exact locations for DPW work orders.
mobility_device_accommodations: Temporary protective rubber matting over any PCI < 40 segments within accessible routes
Manhole cover edge protectors / flap covers on all 22 inventoried covers in event zone
Designated mobility parking zone on Holland Street staging area (closest to Elm St)
Event staff trained in mobility assistance positioned at ADA route entry points
sensory_considerations: Quiet zone area designated away from main stage — recommended near Spoke Wine Bar end of Elm Street (lower traffic density, tree canopy shade from 48 documented trees in radius)
Event signage in large print and high contrast; audio descriptions at information booths
Accessible pedestrian signal push buttons (6 confirmed) audibly guide visually impaired attendees through intersections
Stage audio system design should include hearing loop / FM transmitter for hearing-impaired attendees

shade_and_rest.note: 48 trees inventoried within 500m radius provide natural shade — event layout should cluster seating areas under existing tree canopy along Elm Street
shade_and_rest.seating: Supplement existing benches (detected in imagery: Elm Street at Highland Ave intersection area) with temporary accessible seating (armrests, 17-19" height)

permit_requirements.timeline: Allow up to 6 weeks for approval
requirements: Completed online application with event date, time, location
Written approval from all abutting residents and businesses
1 week written notice to all abutting parties before event
All closure devices supplied by DPW or specified by Parking Department
No private vehicles used as barriers

permit_requirements.application: CitizenServe (combined with Public Event License application)
requirements: Required for any outdoor alcohol service by licensed vendors
Participating bars (The Burren, Foundry on Elm, Painted Burro, Spoke) must apply individually or under event umbrella license
Notification required for any closure affecting MBTA bus routes (87, 89 serve Davis Square area)
MBTA reviews and adjusts routes — contact required before permit finalization
Required for outdoor amplified music or PA systems
Stage must meet decibel limits per Somerville ordinance
Event end time aligned with city noise ordinance
Each food vendor operating outside their licensed premises requires temporary food permit
Inspections may be required for new/temporary kitchen setups
Event layout must demonstrate accessible routes to all activity areas
Cyvl infrastructure data provides compliance documentation baseline
Somerville Commission for Persons with Disabilities should be consulted

crosswalk_pedestrian_search.note: 343 matches indicates robust pedestrian infrastructure documentation across Somerville scan

ada_sidewalk_ramp_search.note: 79 ADA ramp / sidewalk imagery matches support the 21 inventoried RAMP assets

notable_detections: umbrella detected at 42.3975, -71.1234 — near Holland/Elm area (patio/outdoor seating confirmed)
outdoor_seating_search.note: Umbrella detection at Davis Square coordinates confirms existing outdoor dining infrastructure

event_layout_suggestions.main_stage: Center of Elm Street closure, between Highland Ave and Holland St intersections — Good pavement (PCI 85-90 in this zone), existing street lighting (17 luminaries in radius), trees for natural backdrop. Stage faces north toward Davis Square plaza.
event_layout_suggestions.food_and_vendor_zone: South Elm Street block — highest concentration of restaurant frontage; vendors extend existing businesses onto closed street. Protective matting required over any PCI < 55 segments in this zone.
event_layout_suggestions.kids_zone: Holland Street staging area — lower traffic, adjacent to residential neighborhood, Good pavement (PCI 87-89). Set back from main stage for sensory relief.
event_layout_suggestions.quiet_zone: Near Spoke Wine Bar / north end of Elm Street — lower noise exposure, tree canopy from 48-tree inventory, seating area for sensory-sensitive attendees and mobility rest stops.
event_layout_suggestions.bike_corral: Additional temporary bike corrals at College Avenue (MBTA station exit) and Holland Street staging — supplements 1 documented permanent rack. Davis Square attracts high cycling attendance.
event_layout_suggestions.emergency_vehicle_lane: Unobstructed 12-foot lane maintained along Elm Street center, clearly marked and kept clear of vendor tents and crowds.

accessible_restroom_placement.location: College Avenue near MBTA station entrance
accessible_restroom_placement.rationale: First contact point for transit arrivals; highest accessibility need at event entry
Central event access; placed adjacent to existing ADA ramp and level surface

event_layout_suggestions.signage_plan: Temporary wayfinding overlays on existing 200+ inventoried signs; accessible route markers using high-contrast yellow on existing sign poles; dedicated ADA route signage at all 6 push-button signal locations.
event_layout_suggestions.power_infrastructure: 32 utility poles within 500m radius — event electrician can assess tap points for stage power and vendor booth circuits. Coordinate with Eversource for load assessment.
event_layout_suggestions.cyvl_demo_integration: Live Cyvl dashboard displayed at event information booth — showing real-time pavement condition map, sign inventory, and ADA asset locations. Demonstrates how infrastructure intelligence makes the event possible. QR code for Boston CIO and city staff to access data directly.

IMPLAN — Event Impacts: The Economic Value of Community Events

ckan_311.note: Boston 311 dataset covers Boston proper; Somerville maintains separate 311 system. Boston data included for regional context but Davis Square complaints filed through Somerville's CitizenServe system.

demo_narrative_hooks: Cyvl found 2 Failed (PCI < 10) pavement segments within 500 meters of Davis Square — before this event could be safely held, the city needed to know exactly where they were. This is exactly what Cyvl delivered.
21 ADA curb ramps inventoried in the event zone — not guessed, not estimated. The city can guarantee accessible routes because Cyvl mapped every single one.
6 accessible pedestrian push-button signals confirmed at key intersections — the event accessible routing plan follows the infrastructure, not the other way around.
48 trees in the event radius create natural shade zones — Cyvl's above-ground asset inventory let planners design the quiet zone and seating areas around real canopy, not assumptions.
200 signs inventoried and 190 in Good condition — event planners know exactly which 3 Poor-condition signs to replace before thousands of attendees arrive.
343 crosswalk/pedestrian imagery matches across the Somerville dataset — the city can see every intersection, every crossing, and every potential mobility hazard before the event.
The same data that makes this block party safe is the data that helps Somerville prioritize its $50M+ annual pavement repair budget year-round.
